title: Core Python — Variables, Strings, Numbers & Print
intro: Before you write tools that scan networks, you must first write simple programs that print results clearly. Master variables, strings, and numbers. The cybersecurity tools you will build this term are made of these building blocks.

exercises.prompt: Print a two-line banner with a centered title. Line 1: exactly "========================". Line 2: exactly "   Recon Report".

checks.label: Banner prints on two lines with exact spacing

hints: The banner has two lines, so reach for two separate `print()` calls — one per line.
Everything inside the quotes prints exactly as typed, so keep the leading spaces — e.g. `print("   text")` prints three spaces before the word.

lesson.title: Showing output with print()
lesson.body:
`print()` is how a program shows output to the screen. You hand it a value — usually a string of text wrapped in quotes — and it writes that value followed by a newline, so each `print()` call lands on its own line.

Whatever sits between the quotes is printed exactly, character for character. Spaces count: `print("  indented")` keeps its two leading spaces, which is how you align or indent text by hand. Single and double quotes both work as long as the opening and closing marks match.

Calling `print()` several times in a row stacks the lines in the order they run. Printing an empty string, `print("")`, adds a blank line. Reading output carefully is a core skill — every tool you build this term reports its findings through `print()`.

exercises.prompt: Set a variable `name` to "Maya". Using an f-string, print the sentence "Hello, Maya."

checks.label: prints the greeting with the correct name

checks.name: name
checks.label: name variable is set to Maya

hints: First store the text in `name` with `=`, then build the greeting with an f-string that drops `name` in.
An f-string has an `f` before the quote and variables in braces — like `f"Welcome, {user}!"` prints the value of `user`.

lesson.title: Variables and f-strings
lesson.body:
A variable is a name that stores a value so you can reuse it later. You create one with an assignment: a name on the left, an `=`, and a value on the right — for example `port = 80` or `service = "ssh"`. From then on, writing the name gives back the value it holds, and you can reassign it at any time.

An f-string builds text with values dropped into it. Put the letter `f` immediately before the opening quote, then wrap any variable in curly braces `{ }`. Python replaces `{service}` with that variable's current value, while everything outside the braces is printed literally.

f-strings are the clearest way to combine fixed wording with changing data — far more readable than joining fragments by hand. A placeholder can hold any variable or even a small expression, not just strings.

exercises.prompt: Set a variable `ports` to the result of adding 20 and 2. Print the sentence "22 ports" using an f-string.

checks.label: prints 22 ports with the calculated value

hints: Add the two numbers and store the result in `ports`, then print `ports` inside an f-string.
Assign a sum like `total = 5 + 3`, then print it with text using `f"{total} items"`.

lesson.title: Numbers and arithmetic
lesson.body:
Python works with numbers directly, with no quotes around them. The arithmetic operators are `+` (add), `-` (subtract), `*` (multiply), and `/` (divide), and they follow ordinary math precedence, so multiplication happens before addition unless you add parentheses.

A number and a string are different types, and that difference matters. `20 + 2` evaluates to the number `22`, but `"20" + "2"` joins two strings into `"202"`. Keep any value you intend to compute with as a number rather than text.

You can place a calculation directly inside an f-string's braces, such as `f"{8 * 4} bytes"`, and Python prints the computed result. That is convenient for reporting counts, totals, and sizes in a tool's output.

exercises.prompt: You are given two variables: `first="Ada"` and `last="Lovelace"`. Using string concatenation or an f-string, print "Lovelace, Ada" (last name first).
exercises.starter:
first = "Ada"
last = "Lovelace"
# Print last name, first name

checks.label: prints last name followed by first name

hints: Decide the order you want the names to print, then place `last` before `first` with a comma and space between them.
An f-string controls order regardless of how variables were defined — e.g. `f"{b}, {a}"` prints `b` first; or join with `+` like `b + ", " + a`.

lesson.title: Assembling strings in order
lesson.body:
Often you need to build one string from several values arranged in a particular order. With an f-string you list each variable in its own `{ }` and place literal text — spaces, commas, separators — between the braces exactly where it should appear in the output.

The order inside the f-string is the order that prints; you are not bound to the order the variables were created. `f"{b}, {a}"` prints `b` before `a` even if `a` was defined first, which lets you reformat data however a report needs it.

You can also concatenate with the `+` operator, as in `a + ", " + b`, but `+` only joins strings — mixing in a number raises a `TypeError`. f-strings sidestep that pitfall, which is why they are usually the better tool for formatting.

exercises.prompt: Set a variable `count` to `int("443")` (convert the string "443" to an integer). Print the result of `count + 1` using an f-string or concatenation.
hints: The digits are text until you convert them — wrap the string in `int()` so you can do math, then print one more than `count`.
`int()` turns a digit-string into a number, like `n = int("100")`; then you can compute and print with `print(f"{n + 5}")`.

lesson.title: Converting text to numbers with int()
lesson.body:
Text and numbers are distinct types, and digits inside quotes are text. `"443"` is a string of three characters, not the value four hundred forty-three, and you cannot do arithmetic on it while it stays a string.

`int()` converts a string of digits into an integer you can compute with: `int("443")` produces the number `443`, and adding `1` then gives `444`. The opposite conversion, `str()`, turns a number back into text — useful when you want to join it onto other strings.

This conversion comes up constantly in security work. Data read from user input, a file, or the network arrives as text first, so ports, counts, and status codes usually need an `int()` before you can add, compare, or sort them numerically.

stretch.prompt: Stretch: You work with profiles. Set three variables: `name="Jordan"`, `role="analyst"`, and `id="SEC-001"`. Using an f-string, print "Jordan | analyst | SEC-001" with pipes and spaces.
stretch.starter: # Build and print the profile line

checks.label: prints the profile line with correct pipes and spacing

hints: Store each fact in its own variable first, then format all three into one line with a single f-string.
Put each variable in its own braces with the separator as literal text between them — like `f"{a} | {b} | {c}"`.

lesson.title: Formatting several variables at once
lesson.body:
Real tools rarely report a single value — they format several facts into one readable line. Store each fact in its own clearly named variable first; good names make the code read like documentation and are easier to change later.

A single f-string can contain as many `{ }` placeholders as you need, in any order, with literal labels and separators between them. Keeping all the formatting in one f-string makes the output easy to read and adjust in one place.

Composing a tidy summary line from labeled variables is the foundation of the reporting you will do throughout the course — from host profiles to scan results to the final engagement report.
